events.title: A moment.
events.body: You stopped on the walk home for the sunset. Five minutes. Five minutes you would have spent looking at your phone.

events.title: Bank error in your favor.
events.body: A clerical error credits your account by $1,200. Three weeks later, it has not been reversed.

events.title: The office closes.
events.body: An email arrives. Effective immediately. You don't know when you'll be back.

events.title: Skipping breakfast again.
events.body: You realize halfway through the standup that you haven't eaten today. Third time this week.

events.title: An old friend in town.
events.body: Someone you haven't seen in years messages: in town one night. Coffee?
scene: You say yes before you think.

events.title: A post finds its audience.
events.body: Something you wrote a month ago surfaces somewhere. Strangers on the internet are arguing about a paragraph you barely remember writing.

events.title: Return-to-office.
events.body: The email says three days a week, starting next month. The reply-all thread is a study in restraint.

events.title: The tooling lands.
events.body: A new tool eats a task that used to take three days and spits the answer back in twenty minutes. You spend the afternoon staring at the ceiling.

events.title: An invitation.
events.body: A meetup organizer reaches out. Would you give the keynote next month? It pays nothing. It would be good for you.

events.title: A twenty in a coat pocket.
events.body: You pull a jacket out of the closet for the first time since last fall. There is a folded twenty in the inner pocket. You have no memory of putting it there.

events.title: Wrong tab. Wrong button.
events.body: You meant to close a different tab. Forty minutes of work, gone. There was an autosave. You hope.

events.title: A letter from the landlord.
events.body: The lease renewal arrives. The rent is one hundred and twenty dollars higher than last year. The cover letter calls it a 'modest market adjustment.'

events.title: InMail.
events.body: A recruiter at a six-month-old startup messages about a 'unique opportunity.' The company has already pivoted twice. The recruiter, apparently, doesn't know this.

events.title: An old answer ages well.
events.body: A four-year-old answer you wrote on a forum hits a milestone of upvotes. Strangers are still finding it useful. You don't remember writing it.

events.title: Three hours in an airport bar.
events.body: Your flight is delayed three hours. The person at the next stool turns out to be in your industry. By boarding, you've exchanged contact info and a half-baked plan.

events.title: A wallet, briefly missing.
events.body: You left it in a cab. The driver returned it the next day, untouched. You tipped him for the second ride twice as much.

events.title: An annual checkup.
events.body: You went to the doctor for the first time in a while. The blood work came back unremarkable. Unremarkable is the best kind of result.

events.title: A storm and a power outage.
events.body: The power went out at 10pm. You sat in the dark for two hours. You read by phone light. It felt nice not to be reachable.

events.title: Seven calls today.
events.body: Your face has been on a screen for nine hours. You don't remember half of what was decided. You don't think anything was.

events.title: Two desks, one apartment.
events.body: Your roommate's standup is the same time as your morning meeting. You take yours from the bathroom. It's been like this for nine weeks.

events.title: The afternoon walk.
events.body: You started taking a 3pm walk around the block. Then four blocks. Then six. By month three, you knew every dog by name.

events.title: A friend got the call.
events.body: A friend at another company was furloughed. They texted to say they were 'fine, really.' You sent them a long voice memo back. You haven't been furloughed. That feels like more luck than choice.

events.title: Everyone's getting promoted.
events.body: The company's promotion cycle hits hard this year. Two of your peers leveled up. The Slack #promotions channel is louder than usual.

events.title: Comp bands, recalibrated.
events.body: HR sends an email. The bands moved. Your base went up by eight percent. The email used the phrase 'reflecting market realities.'

events.title: Everyone's leaving.
events.body: Three goodbye lunches in one month. Two of them for people you actually liked. The other one had really good cake.

events.title: The juniors are scared.
events.body: A junior asks you, over coffee, whether they should still be doing this. You don't have a clean answer. You give them one anyway.

events.title: A neighboring team gets cut.
events.body: The org chart looks different on Monday. You knew three of them. They posted open-to-work within the week. You wrote three references.

events.title: Everyone's an evangelist.
events.body: A teammate has, this week, recommended the new tooling to you in three separate channels. You've tried it. It's fine. You don't have the heart to say so.

events.title: Ten years.
events.body: You stumble onto a photo from 2020. Your face is different. The kitchen is different. The version of you in the photo could not have predicted the version of you holding the phone.

events.title: Code you wrote, still running.
events.body: Someone messages: that service you wrote five years ago is still in production. It has not been touched. It has not gone down. The new team treats it with religious caution.

events.title: Becoming the person you used to ask.
events.body: A junior asks you a question you remember asking, at the start. You answer it differently than the answer you got. Better, you think.

events.title: Everything old is new.
events.body: A pattern your team mocked in 2022 is the topic of a keynote this week. Three engineers in their twenties are very excited about it. You don't say anything.

events.title: Hiring freeze.
events.body: The annual all-hands is shorter than usual. The word 'discipline' appears three times. Internal mobility is encouraged. External mobility is not.

events.title: A doctor's visit.
events.body: You went in for one thing. The doctor asked about sleep. You answered honestly for the first time in months.

events.title: A cold that lasts ten days.
events.body: It started Monday. By Thursday, you'd cancelled the week. By Sunday, you remembered that lying on the couch was something the body sometimes requires.

events.title: An introduction.
events.body: Someone in your network connects you to someone interesting. No agenda. Just 'I think you two should talk.' You did. You're glad you did.

events.title: Card, declined.
events.body: At the grocery checkout. The cashier said nothing. You used a different card. The people behind you breathed a sigh of relief when the second card worked. 'We've all been there,' someone said.

events.title: An afternoon you'll remember.
events.body: Six hours felt like one. The plan and the code agreed for once. You closed your laptop and went outside while it was still light.

events.title: AM, PM.
events.body: You told your phone to remind you about the 8 AM standup. It heard '8 PM.' You realized this at 8:02 that evening, when the reminder cheerfully went off. Maybe your boss didn't notice. There's always next week's standup.
